data.title: Federal appeals court strikes down most of Trump's tariffs
data.description:
A U.S. appeals court ruled late Friday that most of President Trump's tariffs are illegal, determining the president exceeded his authority in his use of emergency powers to rewrite U.S. trade policy, but the tariffs were allowed to stay in place for now while the case was sent back to a lower court for further proceedings.

The decision from the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit in Washington, D.C., addressed the legality of Trump's "reciprocal" tariffs imposed in April, as well as a separate set of tariffs imposed in February against China, Canada and Mexico, but the ruling does not affect tariffs issued under other legal authority, such as those on steel and aluminum imports.

The court found the president overstepped his authority under a 1977 law known as the International Emergency Economic Powers Act.

The issue of the legality of Trump's has long been expected to end in the U.S. Supreme Court for a final decision.

data.title: Biogen wins approval of at-home version of Alzheimer's drug Leqembi
data.description:
• The U.S. FDA has approved a subcutaneous injection version of Biogen (NASDAQ:BIIB) and Eisai's (OTCPK:ESALF) Alzheimer's therapy Leqembi (lecanemab).
  • A key advantage of the new version, Leqembi IQLIK, is that it can be taken at home. Currently, the treatment is given via an infusion at a healthcare provider's office. 
  • Even with the approval, the new version is only available to those who have been on the infusion dosing regimen for at least 18 months. 
  • Biogen said Leqembi IQLIK will be launched on Oct 6.

data.title: Gold futures finish August at record highs as U.S. inflation data sustains rate cut hopes
data.description:
Gold futures rose Friday, with the front-month Comex contract reaching an all-time closing high, after the latest U.S. inflation data reinforced expectations that the Federal Reserve likely will lower interest rates next month.

The U.S. Commece Department reported the Personal Consumption Expenditure price index - which excludes volatile food and energy prices and is the Fed's preferred inflation gauge - rose 2.9% in the 12 months through July and 0.3% for the month, both in line with analyst estimates and leaving the Fed on track to cut interest rates in September.

Traders raised their bets on a 25-basis-point rate cut at the Fed's September policy meeting to a near 89% probability, up from 85% before the data release.

Gold is trading at the upper end of a range in place since mid-May, Commerzbank analysts said in a note, reflecting Fed Chair Powell's signals about a U.S. interest rate cut, unprecedented political pressure from the White House on the Fed, and persistently high levels of geopolitical tension.

Following President Trump’s move to dismiss Fed Governor Lisa Cook, the market is becoming increasingly concerned that the Fed's independence is being undermined, which analysts say is driving a flight to safe-haven assets like gold.

But Commerzbank believes gold is unlikely to sustain a breach of the recent trading range just yet, as recent political and economic turmoil are relatively unlikely to be repeated and interest rate cut expectations are already mostly priced in.

"Gold is benefiting from this uncertainty [around Fed independence], as shown by inflows into gold ETFs of just under 15 tons in the last two days. Nevertheless, the upside for gold above $3,400 is looking increasingly limited," Commerzbank wrote.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for September delivery ended +1.2% to $3,473.70/oz, a new record close, capping gains of 2.8% this week and 5.5% in August for the metal's best one-month net and percentage gain since April, while the most-active December contract finished +1.2% on Friday and 3% for the week at $3,516.10/oz.

Comex silver (XAGUSD:CUR) continued its strong showing, with the front-month September contract closing +2.6% $40.20/oz, its highest settlement value since September 21, 2011, up 2.9% this week and 10% for August for an eighth straight monthly increase.

data.title: PicoCELA receives Nasdaq notification regarding minimum bid price deficiency
data.description:
• PicoCELA (NASDAQ:PCLA) said on Friday that it has received a letter from Nasdaq, notifying the company that it is not in compliance with the minimum bid price requirement. 
  • Based on the closing bid price of the company's American Depositary Shares for the 30 consecutive business days from July 15, 2025 to August 25, 2025, the company no longer meets the minimum bid price requirement.
  • The company's operations are not affected by the receipt of the notification letter. The company intends to monitor the closing bid price of its ADSs and may consider implementing available options, including, but not limited to, implementing a reverse share split of its outstanding ADSs, to regain compliance.

data.title: Meta set to add new controls after issues over teen safety cop up: report
data.description:
• Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) is set to add new controls and safeguards to its artificial intelligence chatbot after Reuters reported that the app could allow for provocative, sexually explicit or suicidal behavior by teens.
  • The company is taking a number of actions, including temporarily limiting access to certain AI characters, a Meta spokesman told the news outlet. It is also working on long-term initiatives to make sure the teens have “age-appropriate” AI experiences, the news outlet added.
  • Meta shares lost ground on Friday, closing down 1.7%. Shares are up 23.3% for the year.

data.title: Fed is still reviewing Morgan Stanley request for lower stress capital buffer
data.description:
While the Federal Reserve released its list of final capital requirements for large banks, effective Oct. 1, 2025, it's still reviewing Morgan Stanley's (NYSE:MS) request for a downward adjustment in its stress capital buffer, the Wall Street bank said on Friday.

The final calculations were determined by the central bank's annual stress tests, the results of which were released in June. The stress capital buffer is the amount of capital that the largest U.S. banks are required to set aside to weather a potential economic downturn.

"Morgan Stanley (NYSE:MS) remains actively engaged with the Federal Reserve to reach a final SCB requirement before the October 1, 2025 effective date," the company said in a statement.

Morgan Stanley (MS) stock ticked down 0.1% in Friday after-hours trading.
